subject: Physics
q: The weight of a body on the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then weight of the body when taken to a depth half of the radius of the earth will be:
question: The weight of a body on the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then weight of the body when taken to a depth half of the radius of the earth will be:
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity changes with depth from the surface, which is given by

g'=g1-dR   ...(i)

The given data is 

W=400 Nd=R2

Multiplying equation (i) with the mass we get the weight.

mg'=mg1-R2R=mg12⇒W'=400 N2=200 N

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving with a uniform speed in a circular orbit of radius R in a central force inversely proportional to the <math><msup><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> power of R. If the period of rotation of the particle is T, then:
question: A particle is moving with a uniform speed in a circular orbit of radius R in a central force inversely proportional to the <math><msup><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> power of R. If the period of rotation of the particle is T, then:
solution:
F=KRn⇒mV2r=KRn⇒V2∝KRn-1
⇒V∝1Rn-12⇒T=2πRV∝RV
⇒T∝R1Rn-12⇒T∝Rn+12

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass is taken from earth surface to the height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> equal to twice the radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>)</mo></math>, the increase in potential energy will be : (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo></math> acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth)
question: A body of mass is taken from earth surface to the height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> equal to twice the radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>)</mo></math>, the increase in potential energy will be : (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo></math> acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth)
solution:
The potential energy of a particle at a distance r (>Re)  from the centre of Earth is,

U=-GMemr

Initially, the particle is at a height Re from the surface. Therefore, r=Re.

∴Ui=-GMemRe

When height is increased to 2Re from surface, r=2Re+Re=3Re

∴Uf=-GMem3Re

Thus, the change in potential energy is

∆U=Uf-Ui=-GMem3Re+GMemRe=23GMemRe=23GMeRe2mRe=23mgRe as g=GMeRe2

subject: Physics
q: A geostationary satellite is orbiting around an arbitrary planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mi>R</mi></math> above the surface of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> being the radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. The time period of another satellite in hours at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> from the surface of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is ________ has the time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi>hours</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A geostationary satellite is orbiting around an arbitrary planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mi>R</mi></math> above the surface of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> being the radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. The time period of another satellite in hours at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> from the surface of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is ________ has the time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi>hours</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
T∝R3/2

24 T=12R3R3/2⇒T=3 hr

subject: Physics
q: If the distance of the earth from Sun is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the distance of an imaginary planet from Sun, if its period of revolution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>83</mn></math> years is:
question: If the distance of the earth from Sun is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the distance of an imaginary planet from Sun, if its period of revolution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>83</mn></math> years is:
solution:
From Kepler's law of periods, we know that

T2∝R3⇒T1T22=R1R23

⇒12.832=1.5×106R23

⇒R2=2.832×1.5×106313

=813×1.5×106=3×106 km

subject: Physics
q: An object is taken to a height above the surface of earth at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> from the centre of the earth. Where radius of earth, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The percentage decrease in the weight of the object will be
question: An object is taken to a height above the surface of earth at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> from the centre of the earth. Where radius of earth, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The percentage decrease in the weight of the object will be
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at height h is given by, g'=g1+hR2

For distance from the centre r=54R height h=R4,

⇒g'=g1+142=1625g

Change in weight 

∆W=mg-mg'⇒∆W=mg1-1625

⇒∆W=925mg

Percentage change =925×100%

=36%

subject: Physics
q: Two bodies of masses $\mathrm{m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ are placed at a distance $\mathrm{r}$. The gravitational potential at a point on the line joining them where the gravitational field is zero is:
question: Two bodies of masses $\mathrm{m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ are placed at a distance $\mathrm{r}$. The gravitational potential at a point on the line joining them where the gravitational field is zero is:
solution:
Position of the null point from mass $\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{r}}{1+\sqrt{\frac{4 \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{~m}}}}=\frac{r}{3}$
$$
V=-G m\left(\frac{3}{r}+\frac{12}{2 r}\right)=-9 \frac{G m}{r}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A planet having mass 9 Me and radius 4Re, where Me and Re are mass and radius of earth respectively, has escape velocity in km s-1 given by: (Given escape velocity on earth Ve=11.2×103 m s-1)
question: A planet having mass 9 Me and radius 4Re, where Me and Re are mass and radius of earth respectively, has escape velocity in km s-1 given by: (Given escape velocity on earth Ve=11.2×103 m s-1)
solution:
The escape velocity on earth is given by Ve =2GMeRe

Escape velocity for the planet is  

V'esc=2G(9Me)4Re   ...(i)

The escape velocity of earth is Ve=11.2×103 m s-1

From equation (i)

V'esc=942GMeRe=32×11.2×103 m s-1⇒V'esc=16.8 km s-1

subject: Physics
q: The gravitational potential at a point above the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the acceleration due to gravity at that point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Assume that the mean radius of earth to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The height of this point above the earth's surface is:
question: The gravitational potential at a point above the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the acceleration due to gravity at that point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Assume that the mean radius of earth to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The height of this point above the earth's surface is:
solution:
Gravitational potential at a distance r from the centre of the earth is given by, V=-GMr.

Therefore, we can write

-GMERE+h=-5.12×107    …i

also acceleration due to gravity at that point will be

GMERE+h2=6.4    …ii

By (i) and (ii), we get

-GMERE+hGMERE+h2=5.12×1076.4

⇒RE+h=80×105 m=8000 km⇒h=8000 km-6400 km=1600 km

subject: Physics
q:
A body is projected vertically upwards from the surface of earth with a velocity equal to one third of escape velocity. The maximum height attained by the body will be
(Take radius of earth =6400 km and g=10 ms-2)
question:
A body is projected vertically upwards from the surface of earth with a velocity equal to one third of escape velocity. The maximum height attained by the body will be
(Take radius of earth =6400 km and g=10 ms-2)
solution:
Total mechanical energy of the body at the surface of earth

Mi=-GMmR+12m132GMR2=-GMmR+GMm9R=-8GMm9R

Total mechanical energy of the body at the highest point

Mf=-GMmR+h

Using conservation of mechanical energy,

Mi=Mf⇒-GMmR+h=-8GMm9R

⇒R+h=9R8

⇒h=R8=64008=800 km

subject: Physics
q:
The approximate height from the surface of earth at which the weight of the body becomes 13 of its weight on the surface of earth is :
[Radius of earth R=6400 km and 3=1.732]
question:
The approximate height from the surface of earth at which the weight of the body becomes 13 of its weight on the surface of earth is :
[Radius of earth R=6400 km and 3=1.732]
solution:
The variation of the acceleration due to gravity with height h is given by,

g'=g1+hR2.

Since the weight at given height is 13 of the weight at the surface

⇒g'=g3⇒g1+hR2=g3⇒1+hR=3⇒h=R3-1=6400×1.732-1=4685 km

subject: Physics
q: The height in terms of radius of the earth $(R)$, at which the acceleration due to gravity becomes $\dfrac{g}{9}$, where $g$ is acceleration due to gravity on earth's surface, is ______.
question: The height in terms of radius of the earth $(R)$, at which the acceleration due to gravity becomes $\dfrac{g}{9}$, where $g$ is acceleration due to gravity on earth's surface, is ______.
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity at a height $h$ above the surface of the earth is given by

$g' = \dfrac{g}{\left(1 + \dfrac{h}{R}\right)^2}$

Given that $g' = \dfrac{g}{9}$, we get

$\dfrac{g}{9} = \dfrac{g}{\left(1 + \dfrac{h}{R}\right)^2}$

$\left(1 + \dfrac{h}{R}\right)^2 = 9$

$1 + \dfrac{h}{R} = 3$

$\dfrac{h}{R} = 2$

$h = 2R$

Answer: $2R$

subject: Physics
q: The time period of a satellite, revolving above earth's surface at a height equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> will be (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of earth)
question: The time period of a satellite, revolving above earth's surface at a height equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> will be (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of earth)
solution:
The velocity of the satellite revolving round the Earth at a height r from the centre of the Earth is given by

v=GMr   ...1

The formula to calculate the time period in the circular orbit is given by

T=2πrv   ...2

In this case: r=R+R=2R

Substitute the expression for the velocity from equation (1) into equation (2) and simplify to obtain the required time period.

T=2πrGMr=2π×2RgR2=4πR2πR=42R=32R

subject: Physics
q: If a satellite orbiting the Earth is 9 times closer to the Earth than the Moon, what is the time period of rotation of the satellite? Given rotational time period of Moon $=27$ days and gravitational attraction between the satellite and the moon is neglected.
question: If a satellite orbiting the Earth is 9 times closer to the Earth than the Moon, what is the time period of rotation of the satellite? Given rotational time period of Moon $=27$ days and gravitational attraction between the satellite and the moon is neglected.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}^2 \propto \mathrm{R}^3 \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{s}}}\right)^2=\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R} / 9}\right)^3 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{s}}}=(3)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{s}}=\left(\frac{27}{27}\right)=1 \text { day }\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : Rotation of the earth shows effect on the value of acceleration due to gravity (g).

Statement II : The effect of rotation of the earth on the value of g at the equator is minimum and that at the pole is maximum.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : Rotation of the earth shows effect on the value of acceleration due to gravity (g).

Statement II : The effect of rotation of the earth on the value of g at the equator is minimum and that at the pole is maximum.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is false but statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but statement II is false
solution:
The variation of acceleration due to gravity with the rotation of Earth can be written as

g'=g-ω2Rcos2λ   ...1

Substitute 0° for λ for equatorial position to obtain the acceleration due to gravity.

g'=g-ω2Rcos20°= g-ω2R

Substitute 90° for λ for polar position to obtain the acceleration due to gravity.

g'=g-ω2Rcos290°=g

The above two equations show that the effect on acceleration due to gravity is maximum at the equator and minimum at pole.

subject: Physics
q: Correct formula for height of a satellite from earths surface is:
question: Correct formula for height of a satellite from earths surface is:
solution: $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})^2}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{GM}}{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})}=\mathrm{v}^2$ ....(1) $\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h}) \omega$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h}) \frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}}$ ....(2) $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}^2}=\mathrm{g}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{GM}=\mathrm{gR}^2$ ....(3) Put value from (2) \& (3) in eq. (1) $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{gR}^2}{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})}=(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})^2\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}}\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}^2 \mathrm{R}^2 \mathrm{~g}}{(2 \pi)^2}=(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})^3 \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\frac{\mathrm{T}^2 \mathrm{R}^2 \mathrm{~g}}{(2 \pi)^2}\right]^{1 / 3}-\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{h} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two planets, $A$ and $B$ are orbiting a common star in circular orbits of radii $R_A$ and $R_B$, respectively, with $R_B=2 R_A$. The planet $B$ is $4 \sqrt{2}$ times more massive than planet $A$. The ratio $\left(\frac{L_B}{L_A}\right)$ of angular momentum $\left(L_B\right)$ of planet $B$ to that of planet $A\left(L_A\right)$ is closest to integer ________.
question: Two planets, $A$ and $B$ are orbiting a common star in circular orbits of radii $R_A$ and $R_B$, respectively, with $R_B=2 R_A$. The planet $B$ is $4 \sqrt{2}$ times more massive than planet $A$. The ratio $\left(\frac{L_B}{L_A}\right)$ of angular momentum $\left(L_B\right)$ of planet $B$ to that of planet $A\left(L_A\right)$ is closest to integer ________.
solution:
$\mathrm{L}=\mathrm{mv}_0 \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{m} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}}} \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{m} \sqrt{\mathrm{GMR}}$
here M is mass of star
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{A}}} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}}} \\
& =4 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{\frac{2}{1}} \\
& \frac{\mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{A}}}=8
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement-I: Acceleration due to gravity is different at different places on the surface of earth.

Statement-II: Acceleration due to gravity increases as we go down below the earth's surface.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement-I: Acceleration due to gravity is different at different places on the surface of earth.

Statement-II: Acceleration due to gravity increases as we go down below the earth's surface.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity is the acceleration acquired by a freely falling object.

At any particular place on the Earth, the acceleration due to gravity depends on the latitude of the place and the angular frequency of Earth's rotation. Mathematically, the latitude dependence of acceleration due to gravity can be written as

geff= g-Rω2cos2λ.................(1)

where, geff is the effective acceleration due to gravity, g is the real value of gravity at the latitude without considering earth rotation, R is the radius of Earth, ω is the angular frequency of rotation and λ is the latitude of the place.

According to equation (1), the value of the acceleration due to gravity varies from place to place.

The variation of acceleration due to gravity with the depth can be written as

gd= g1-dR....................(2)

where, gd is the acceleration due to gravity at a depth d from the surface and g is the acceleration due to gravity at the surface.

From equation (2), it can be stated that the acceleration due to gravity decreases with the depth.

Hence, Statement I is true, but statement II is false.

subject: Physics
q: The radius in kilometer to which the present radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>)</mo></math> to be compressed so that the escape velocity is increased <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> time is _______.
question: The radius in kilometer to which the present radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>)</mo></math> to be compressed so that the escape velocity is increased <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> time is _______.
solution:
Ve=2GmR …1

10 Ve=2GmR' …2

∴10=RR'

⇒R'=R100=6400100=64 km

q: The mass density of a spherical galaxy varies as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="italic">K</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">r</mi></mfrac></math> over a large distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> from its center. In that region, a small star is in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. Then the period of revolution,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> depends on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> as:
question: The mass density of a spherical galaxy varies as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="italic">K</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">r</mi></mfrac></math> over a large distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> from its center. In that region, a small star is in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. Then the period of revolution,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> depends on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> as:
solution:
M=∫ρdV

M=∫0Rkr4πr2dr

M=4πk∫0Rrdr

M=4πkR22=2πkR2

FG=GMmR2=mω02R

⇒  G4πkR22R2=ω02R  ⇒ω0=2πkGR

∵T=2πω0=2πR2πkG=2πRkG

⇒T2∝R

subject: Physics
q: A satellite is in an elliptical orbit around a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is observed that the velocity of the satellite when it is farthest from the planet is 6 times less than that when it is closest to the planet. The ratio of distances between the satellite and the planet at closest and farthest points is :
question: A satellite is in an elliptical orbit around a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is observed that the velocity of the satellite when it is farthest from the planet is 6 times less than that when it is closest to the planet. The ratio of distances between the satellite and the planet at closest and farthest points is :
solution:
From angular momentum conservation

mV1r1=mV2r2

r1r2=v2v1=16

1:6

subject: Physics
q: For a body projected at an angle with the horizontal from the ground, choose the correct statement
question: For a body projected at an angle with the horizontal from the ground, choose the correct statement
options: Gravitational potential energy is maximum at the highest point.
The horizontal component of velocity is zero at highest point.
The vertical component of momentum is maximum at the highest point.
The kinetic energy (K.E.) is zero at the highest point of projectile motion.
solution:
In a projectile motion, a particle has two components of velocity. The horizontal component of velocity remains constant over time, while the vertical component of velocity changes.

At highest point, the vertical component of velocity vy becomes zero, but the horizontal component is non-zero. So the kinetic energy of the particle is not zero.

The formula to calculate the potential energy U of the particle is given by

U= mgh...................(1)

where, m is the mass, g is the acceleration due to gravity and h is the vertical height attained by the object.

Hence, the potential energy is maximum when the projectile attains the maximum height.

subject: Physics
q:
The ratio of escape velocity of a planet to the escape velocity of earth will be:-

Given: Mass of the planet is 16 times mass of earth and radius of the planet is 4 times the radius of earth.
question:
The ratio of escape velocity of a planet to the escape velocity of earth will be:-

Given: Mass of the planet is 16 times mass of earth and radius of the planet is 4 times the radius of earth.
solution:
The formula to calculate the escape velocity is given by

ve=2GMR   ...1

For the Earth, it is given by

veE=2GMERE...2

 and, for the planet, it is given by

veP=2GMPRP...3

Divide equation (2) by equation (3) and simplify to obtain the required ratio.

veEveP=MEMP×RPRE   ...4

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (4) to obtain the required ratio.

veEveP=ME16ME×4RERE=12⇒veP:veE= 2:1

subject: Physics
q: The weight of a body on the surface of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The gravitational force on it when taken at a height, from the surface of earth, equal to one-fourth the radius of the earth is:
question: The weight of a body on the surface of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The gravitational force on it when taken at a height, from the surface of earth, equal to one-fourth the radius of the earth is:
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity changes as one goes to a height from the surface of the earth. Let the new acceleration due to gravity be g'. The formula for acceleration due to gravity at a height is given by g'=gR2R+h2

The weight of the body on surface is W=mg=100 N

The weight at the given height is W=mg'.

⇒W'=100×R2R+R42⇒W'=100×R225R216=64 N

subject: Physics
q: The value of the acceleration due to gravity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of the earth) from the surface of the earth. It is again equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> below the surface the earth. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>R</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> equals:
question: The value of the acceleration due to gravity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of the earth) from the surface of the earth. It is again equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> below the surface the earth. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>R</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> equals:
solution:
Given that

gn=gd

GM(R+h)2=GMR3(R-d)

GM(R+R/2)2=GMR3(R-d)

4GM9R2=GMR21-dR

49=1-dR

dR=1-49=59

d=59R

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: If E be the total energy of a satellite moving around the earth, then its potential energy will be  E2.

Statement II: The kinetic energy of a satellite revolving in an orbit is equal to the half the magnitude of total energy E.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: If E be the total energy of a satellite moving around the earth, then its potential energy will be  E2.

Statement II: The kinetic energy of a satellite revolving in an orbit is equal to the half the magnitude of total energy E.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
solution:
Let m be the mass of the satellite.

The potential energy of the satellite is 

U=-GMmr

The velocity of the satellite is given by 

v=GMr

Kinetic energy is given by

K=mv22=m2GMr=GMm2r

The total mechanical energy is the sum of potential and kinetic energy. So,

T=K+U=GMm2r-GMmr=-GMm2r

Hence, both the statements are false.

q: In the reported figure of earth, the value of acceleration due to gravity is same at point A and C but it is smaller than that of its value at point B (surface of the earth). The value of OA:AB will be x:5. The value of x is
question: In the reported figure of earth, the value of acceleration due to gravity is same at point A and C but it is smaller than that of its value at point B (surface of the earth). The value of OA:AB will be x:5. The value of x is
solution:
gA=GMrR3

gC=GMR+R22
gA=gC

rR3=194R2⇒r=4R9

so OA=4R9; AB=R-r=5R9

OA:AB=4:5

subject: Physics
q: Three masses $200 \mathrm{~kg}, 300 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 400 kg are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle with sides 20 m. They are rearranged on the vertices of a bigger triangle of side 25 m and with the same centre. The work done in this process $\_\_\_\_$ J. (Gravitational constant $\mathrm{G}=6.7 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{kg}^{2}$)
question: Three masses $200 \mathrm{~kg}, 300 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 400 kg are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle with sides 20 m. They are rearranged on the vertices of a bigger triangle of side 25 m and with the same centre. The work done in this process $\_\_\_\_$ J. (Gravitational constant $\mathrm{G}=6.7 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{kg}^{2}$)
solution:
Work done equals negative change in gravitational potential energy.
For three masses in an equilateral triangle, gravitational PE is $U = -G\left(\frac{m_1m_2}{r_{12}} + \frac{m_2m_3}{r_{23}} + \frac{m_1m_3}{r_{13}}\right)$.
Initial configuration (side 20 m):
$U_i = -\frac{G}{20}(200 \times 300 + 300 \times 400 + 200 \times 400)$
$= -\frac{260000G}{20} = -13000G$
Final configuration (side 25 m):
$U_f = -\frac{G}{25}(260000) = -10400G$
Work done:
$W = -(U_f - U_i) = -10400G + 13000G$
$= 2600G = 2600 \times 6.7 \times 10^{-11}$
$= 1.742 \times 10^{-7}$ J $\approx 1.74 \times 10^{-7}$ J

q: Three identical particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> each are placed in a straight line with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The gravitational force on a fourth particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of the same mass is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, when placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> on the perpendicular bisector of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> will be approximately
question: Three identical particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> each are placed in a straight line with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The gravitational force on a fourth particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of the same mass is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, when placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> on the perpendicular bisector of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> will be approximately
solution:
Given here, m=100 kg

Gravitational force, FAP=Gm21322,

FBP=Gm2132 and FCP=Gm21322

Net gravitational force, 

Fnet=FBP+FAPcos45°+FCPcos45°

 =G×1041321+12

F≃100G

subject: Physics
q:
Inside a uniform spherical shell :

(a) The gravitational field is zero.

(b) The gravitational potential is zero.

(c) The gravitational field is the same everywhere.

(d) The gravitation potential is the same everywhere.

(e) All the above.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Inside a uniform spherical shell :

(a) The gravitational field is zero.

(b) The gravitational potential is zero.

(c) The gravitational field is the same everywhere.

(d) The gravitation potential is the same everywhere.

(e) All the above.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
By using graphical representation of gravitational potential due to a uniform spherical shell and radius of the shell, we can see inside the shell the gravitational potential is not zero, and the gravitational potential is same everywhere inside the shell.

 

By using graphical representation of gravitational field due to a uniform spherical shell and radius of the shell, we can see the gravitational field inside the shell is zero.
